continue: Training status just flipped to “Productive” — your VO2 work is registering. HRV is still climbing (56 ms, balanced) and resting HR is low (48). The engine is responding; hold one hard quality day per week.
You absorbed the buildup well — 8.5 h sleep last night (score 89), balanced stress, low resting HR. Recovery capacity is solid.
Last 7 days hit ~27 mi — your highest in a while and a real step toward the 20s–30s. Good direction; keep the added volume easy.
Resting HR is excellent and stable (46–53, 7-day avg 48) — a strong aerobic-fitness signal. Keep easy days truly easy so it stays low.
change: Your polarized split is inverted — over the last 30 days only 20% of your miles were truly easy while 56% sat in the gray zone (avg HR ~139–151) and 24% hard. Polarized training wants ~80% easy / ~20% hard with almost no gray. This is the #1 fix and the likely root of the VO2max plateau: slow your easy runs to HR <135 so they actually recover you — which lets your hard days go genuinely hard. (Note: today's interval session is counted as “gray” here because it's classified by each run's average HR — a known limitation; it was a true hard session.)

Load spiked after today's intervals — ACWR jumped to 1.5 (“High”) and post-workout readiness dropped to 34. That's normal for a hard session, but the next 2–3 days should be genuinely easy. Your scheduled Friday easy-Z2 and Sunday long runs are exactly right — resist adding intensity until ACWR settles back toward ~1.0.

Garmin still flags “Aerobic Low Shortage” — you need more easy Z2 volume, not more intensity. Keep ramping weekly mileage toward the 20s–30s; that aerobic base is what raises the VO2max ceiling alongside the intervals.
Sleep duration still swings (6.6 h → 8.5 h). Last night was great; aim for a consistent 7.5 h+ with a steady bedtime — consistency beats the occasional long night.

Weight is trending down — 173.2 lb (BMI 24.2), down 1.2 lb from your first log on Jun 17. ~10 lb above your low-160s goal; hold the sustainable ~1 lb/week pace and keep logging weigh-ins so BMI and fitness age keep tracking.
